title: Too Good to Be True
introduction: Morgan has been chatting online with someone called 'Daniel' for three weeks. Read this conversation. Tap anything suspicious, then press Submit.

script.text: I've been thinking about you all day 😊 You're honestly so different from everyone else I've talked to. You really get me in a way nobody else does. I've never felt this connected to someone so quickly.
script.explanation: Romance scammers deliberately build intense emotional bonds fast — a technique called 'love bombing'. Three weeks in and feeling like a uniquely special connection is a warning sign, not a green flag. Real relationships develop more gradually.

script.text: I feel the same way! When are we going to meet?
I want that so much. I'm working on an oil rig right now but once this contract ends in 6 weeks I'm flying straight to you. I've already been looking at flights. I just want to hold you 💙
script.explanation: Romance scammers are always in a location that prevents meeting — oil rigs, military deployment, working abroad. The meeting is always 'coming soon' but never happens. This maintains the relationship without requiring in-person verification.

script.text: Can we video call? I'd love to see your face.
The internet connection out here is terrible for video — it keeps cutting out. I've tried but it never works on the rig. I'll send you a photo though 📸 [sends photo of attractive stranger]
script.flag_label: Refuses video call, sends photos instead
script.explanation: Scammers use stolen photos of real people. Video calls would immediately reveal they're not the person in the photo — hence the permanent excuse. Oil rigs have satellite internet capable of video calls. This excuse is a standard part of the script.

script.text: I hate to ask this but I'm in a really difficult situation. There's been an accident on the rig and I need emergency medical treatment. My company should cover it but there's a delay in the insurance and I need €800 to pay the hospital now or they won't treat me. Can you help? I promise I'll pay you back the moment I land.
script.explanation: This is the moment the entire relationship was building towards. The emotional investment of three weeks makes this request feel impossible to refuse. Medical emergencies, legal problems, travel costs — the specific crisis varies but the request is always money, always urgent, and the repayment never comes.

learning_objectives: Love bombing — intense connection building very quickly — is a manipulation tactic
Someone who is always unavailable for video calls is hiding their identity
Photos can be stolen — a photo is not proof of identity
A financial request after weeks of emotional investment is the goal of the entire relationship
Report romance scammers to Action Fraud (UK) or Gardaí (Ireland)
debrief:
Romance scams cause serious financial and emotional harm. Victims often lose thousands — and feel ashamed, which prevents reporting.

**There is no shame in being targeted.** These scammers are professional manipulators who run dozens of these 'relationships' simultaneously, using scripts refined over years.

**The pattern is always the same:** Build emotional connection → prevent verification → introduce financial crisis → request money → repeat until victim runs out or realises.

**If you think you're in this situation:** Stop all money transfers immediately. Don't feel embarrassed — report it. In the UK, contact Action Fraud at actionfraud.police.uk. In Ireland, contact your local Gardaí station.
